rationale: In the introduction and hypothesis setup, the citing paper uses Shang, Reed, and Croson (2008) to support a specific claim that social influence may depend on the social identity match between prior and focal consumers (e.g., male vs female prior buyers). It also cites this work when justifying gender as a relevant identity marker for behavioral effects. Even though the citation is grouped with other references and not used for methods, it informs a central theoretical moderator the paper tests, so it is more than a passing mention.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Shang et al.'s identity-congruency social influence idea from charitable giving to automobile purchases. It examines analogous sender-receiver gender effects and identity relevance in a different, high-stakes market context.
